race.name: RAIN - Ride Across Indiana
race.tagline: 160 miles in one day across the entire state of Indiana. One road, one direction, dawn to dusk. America's most honest century-plus ride.

vitals.location: Terre Haute to Richmond, Indiana, USA
vitals.registration: Online via GetMeRegistered.com or centralincycling.org. Registration opens January/March. Non-refundable and non-transferable.
vitals.prize_purse: None. Not a race. Keychains awarded to all finishers by 9 PM.
vitals.aid_stations: 5 rest stops during the ride, one including lunch. Food at the finish. All staffed by volunteers from the Bloomington Bicycle Club and Central Indiana Cycling.
vitals.cutoff_time: 14 hours (must finish by 9 PM for finisher award)

climate.description: Late June/July in central Indiana is brutally honest weather. Temperatures routinely reach 85-95F with high humidity. The course on US 40 and county roads offers minimal shade -- open farmland stretches for miles without a tree. Morning starts at 7 AM are in the 65-75F range but the temperature climbs steadily. By noon, you are baking on exposed asphalt. Thunderstorms can roll in without much warning, delivering torrential rain, lightning, and sudden temperature drops. Wind across the Indiana flatlands can create sustained headwinds or crosswinds with nothing to block them.
challenges: 85-95F temperatures with high Midwest humidity by midday
160 miles of exposed farmland with minimal shade
Afternoon thunderstorm risk: lightning, heavy rain, gusty winds
Sustained crosswinds across open Indiana flatlands
Heat exhaustion risk over 10-14 hours of riding in summer conditions

terrain.primary: Flat to gently rolling paved roads across Indiana farmland
terrain.surface: Paved roads throughout. Primarily the Historic National Road (US 40), a divided four-lane highway with light traffic and gentle rolling hills. The 2026 route adds more county roads to get off US 40 and onto roads with better paving. Surface quality varies from smooth county roads to rough patches on US 40. No gravel, no off-road. The terrain is flat by any meaningful standard: Indiana is not known for hills.
features: Historic National Road (US 40): America's first federally funded highway
Gentle rolling Indiana farmland: minimal elevation change
St. Mary-of-the-Woods College start (historical) / Fairbanks Park start (2026)
160 miles of Midwest heartland scenery: cornfields, small towns, covered bridges

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: RAIN is not about terrain, climbing, or technical challenge. It is about distance and endurance in honest Midwest conditions. 160 miles in a single day on flat roads in summer heat is a pure test of sustained effort and willpower. The value (5) is the highest possible for a ride that costs $60-$80 and delivers 5 rest stops and a finish-line meal.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: Length (5) is the maximum for 160 miles in a single day -- one of America's longest one-day rides. Value (5) for extraordinary bang-per-buck at $60-$80. Community (4) for 37 years of Bloomington Bicycle Club volunteer culture. Technicality (1) and elevation (1) are rock-bottom because Indiana is flat and the roads are straight. Altitude (1) because the highest point on course is approximately 1,100 feet.

logistics.explanation: The point-to-point format from Terre Haute to Richmond (160 miles apart) creates genuine logistical complexity. You need to arrange transportation back to Terre Haute, or have someone drop you off and pick you up in Richmond. Indianapolis International (IND) is the closest major airport, 75 miles from the start. Terre Haute has limited accommodation. Personal SAG vehicles are allowed and common. The 2026 event starts at Fairbanks Park in Terre Haute. Packet pickup is at the start the morning of the ride.

length.explanation: 160 miles in a single day. This is the defining characteristic of RAIN and the reason people ride it. At a rolling average of 14 mph (the minimum recommended), that is 11.5 hours of pedaling. Most riders take 10-14 hours including stops. The 2026 addition of a 210-mile ultra option pushes into legitimately extreme territory. This is not a century ride -- it is a century ride with another 60 miles bolted on. You will be on the bike from dawn until dusk.

technicality.explanation: This is the flattest, most technically unchallenging ride in the database. Indiana. Flat roads. Straight lines. US 40 is a divided four-lane highway. The county road additions in 2026 add some gentle rolling but nothing that would register as a hill in any other state. If you can pedal a bicycle in a straight line for 14 hours, you have all the technical skill this ride requires.

elevation.explanation: Approximately 3,000 feet of total elevation gain over 160 miles. That is less than 20 feet per mile, which is essentially flat. There are no climbs. There are gentle rollers that barely register on an elevation profile. The challenge here is not climbing -- it is the relentless, grinding, unshaded miles across the Indiana plain. The terrain does not fight you vertically. It fights you horizontally, with distance.

climate.explanation: Late June/July in Indiana is hot, humid, and unshaded. Temperatures of 85-95F with 60-80% humidity are normal. The course crosses open farmland with zero shade for miles at a stretch. Afternoon thunderstorms can deliver torrential rain and lightning. The heat is the ride's true adversary: over 10-14 hours, even moderate temperatures accumulate into a debilitating heat load. RAIN has earned its reputation partly because riders discover that 160 flat miles in Indiana summer heat is harder than they imagined.

altitude.explanation: The entire course sits between 700 and 1,100 feet above sea level. Altitude is completely irrelevant. You will not even think about it. This is as close to sea-level riding as you can get without actually being at the coast.

adventure.explanation: Riding across an entire state in a single day is inherently adventurous, even if the terrain is flat. You start in Terre Haute as the sun comes up and finish in Richmond as it goes down. The Historic National Road carries 200 years of American transportation history. Small Indiana towns welcome riders through their main streets. Cornfields stretch to the horizon. It is not dramatic scenery, but the sheer scale of crossing a state under your own power creates a genuine sense of journey.

prestige.explanation: RAIN is in its 37th year (2026), making it one of the longest-running cycling events in America. Named a Midwest Best-in-Class event by Strambecco. The Bloomington Bicycle Club has run this event since 1989 (now transitioned to Central Indiana Cycling). Within the Midwest cycling community, RAIN is iconic -- a rite of passage. Nationally, it is known among ultra-distance enthusiasts. Internationally, it is obscure.

race_quality.explanation: RAIN is not a race -- it is a ride. But it is a well-organized ride after 37 years of refinement. 5 rest stops stocked by volunteers, one with a full lunch. SAG vehicles on course. Medical support. Personal SAG vehicles are allowed and common. The volunteer infrastructure from the Bloomington Bicycle Club is deep and committed. The 2026 route improvements (more county roads, better paving) show continued investment in the rider experience.

experience.explanation: The experience of RAIN is not about spectacular scenery or technical thrills. It is about the meditative, grinding, honest work of riding 160 miles across flat farmland in summer heat. You watch the sun cross the sky from east to west. You pass through small towns that exist in a different century. You discover what your body and mind can do over 10-14 hours. The finish at Earlham College in Richmond -- food, a keychain medal, the disbelief of completion -- is earned in the most fundamental way possible. It is a purer cycling experience than most flashy events can offer.

community.explanation: 37 years of the Bloomington Bicycle Club (now Central Indiana Cycling) running this event has created a deep, loyal community. 2,000-3,000 riders who choose to ride 160 miles across Indiana are a self-selecting group of determined, friendly people. The Facebook group is active year-round. The volunteer culture is genuine -- these are cyclists helping cyclists. The small-town communities along the route welcome riders with genuine Midwest hospitality. Results going back to 1996 are publicly available.

field_depth.explanation: RAIN is explicitly not a race. There are no start waves, no chip timing, no competitive categories, no prizes beyond the finisher keychain. Riders are expected to maintain a rolling average of at least 14 mph, which is a survival pace, not a competitive standard. The field ranges from ultra-distance specialists to charity riders to people testing their limits for the first time. There is no competitive depth because there is no competition.

value.explanation: $60-$80 registration includes a rider t-shirt, rider flag, 5 rest stops (one with lunch), food at the finish, and a finisher keychain. This is a totally volunteer-operated event. After expenses, funds go to townships along the route, a local grant program, and club events. For a 160-mile, fully-supported ride, this is the best value in American cycling. You cannot ride 160 miles with 5 staffed rest stops anywhere else for less.

expenses.explanation: $60-$80 registration. Terre Haute accommodation is $60-$120/night for basic hotels. Richmond accommodation is similar. Food is Midwest-priced: affordable. You need transportation between Terre Haute and Richmond (or a personal SAG driver). No flights needed for Midwest riders. Total cost from the Midwest: $150-$300. From outside the region: $500-$1,000 with flights. This is not a luxury event. It is a ride that costs what it costs and delivers exactly what it promises.

biased_opinion.summary:
RAIN strips cycling down to its purest elements: you, a bike, 160 miles, and the entire state of Indiana between point A and point B. There are no mountains to conquer, no technical sections to navigate, no exotic destinations to photograph. There is a flat road, a summer sun, and the question of whether you can keep pedaling for 10 to 14 hours.

The Historic National Road (US 40) is not beautiful in the way that the Sea-to-Sky Highway or the Dolomites are beautiful. It is beautiful in the way that honesty is beautiful. Cornfields. Small towns. A road that stretches to the horizon. The sun tracking across the sky as you track across the state. You start in Terre Haute at 7 AM and you finish in Richmond sometime before 9 PM, and in between you discover what 160 miles of flat road in Indiana summer heat actually feels like.

It feels harder than you expected. The heat accumulates. The miles accumulate. The lack of climbing means you never get a descent to rest on. You pedal every inch. At $60-$80 with 5 rest stops and a volunteer army, it is the best value in American cycling. RAIN does not pretend to be anything it is not. It is 160 miles. It is flat. It is hot. It is honest. And it has been doing this for 37 years.
strengths: 160 miles in a single day: one of America's longest one-day rides
Best value in American cycling: $60-$80 for 5 rest stops and full support
37 years of volunteer-driven tradition from the Bloomington Bicycle Club
Pure endurance test: no climbing gimmicks, no technical distractions, just miles
weaknesses: Indiana summer heat (85-95F) with 160 miles of exposed, shadeless farmland
Flat terrain makes this a grinding endurance test, not a dramatic cycling experience
Point-to-point logistics: you need transportation between Terre Haute and Richmond
Not a race: no competition, no chip timing, no prizes
biased_opinion.bottom_line: RAIN is 160 miles of truth. No gimmicks, no glamour, no mountains. Just a flat road across Indiana in the summer heat and the question of whether you can make it from dawn to dusk. At $60-$80, it costs less than most 50-mile charity rides and delivers three times the distance.

final_verdict.one_liner: 160 miles across Indiana in a single day. Flat, hot, honest, and the best $60 you will spend on a bike.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want a pure ultra-distance endurance challenge on flat roads, appreciate volunteer-driven cycling culture, and can handle Indiana summer heat. Skip if you need mountains, technical terrain, competition, or Instagram-worthy scenery.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a similar ultra-distance flat ride: BikeMI (Michigan). For Midwest climbing: Hilly Hundred (Indiana, fall). For a shorter state-crossing ride: Ride the Rockies (Colorado, multi-day).

logistics.airport: Indianapolis International (IND), 75 miles from the start in Terre Haute. Dayton International (DAY), 45 miles from the finish in Richmond.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Terre Haute hotels for the night before (Holiday Inn, Drury Inn, etc. at $60-$120/night). Richmond hotels for the night after if not driving home. Many riders arrange personal SAG vehicles that drive to Richmond and meet them at the finish. Group carpools from Indianapolis are common.
logistics.food: Midwest diner fare in Terre Haute and Richmond. Nothing fancy, nothing expensive. Rest stop food during the ride is the highlight: volunteer-prepared, well-stocked, and genuine. Lunch is provided at one of the five rest stops.
logistics.packet_pickup: Morning of the event at the start in Fairbanks Park, Terre Haute. Pre-ride briefing at 6:55 AM, ride starts at 7:00 AM.
logistics.parking: Fairbanks Park in Terre Haute has start-area parking. Many riders arrange drop-off and pick-up to avoid the point-to-point parking challenge.

history.origin_story: RAIN was born in 1989 as a one-day challenge organized by the Bloomington Bicycle Club: ride across the entire state of Indiana in a single day, from West Terre Haute to Richmond, following the Historic National Road (US 40). The concept was simple and has barely changed in 37 years. The ride grew through word-of-mouth in the Midwest cycling community, attracting riders who wanted to test their endurance on a grand scale. In 2026, Central Indiana Cycling took over operations and added a 210-mile ultra option. The event remains entirely volunteer-operated, with proceeds supporting communities along the route.
notable_moments: 1989: Inaugural RAIN ride from West Terre Haute to Richmond on the Historic National Road
2000s: Event grows to 2,000+ riders, establishing itself as a Midwest cycling institution
2024: Named Midwest Best-in-Class event by Strambecco
2026: Central Indiana Cycling takes over operations. Route updated with more county roads. 210-mile ultra option added. Start moves to Fairbanks Park.
history.reputation: The Midwest's ultimate endurance ride. 37 years of crossing Indiana from dawn to dusk.

course_description.character: A straight line across Indiana. The course follows the Historic National Road (US 40), America's first federally funded highway, from Terre Haute in the west to Richmond in the east. The terrain is flat by any standard. The challenge is pure distance and the Indiana summer conditions.

suffering_zones.label: Terre Haute Start
suffering_zones.desc: 7 AM start at Fairbanks Park. Cool morning air, nervous energy, 160 miles ahead. The first miles through Terre Haute are urban and easy. The day feels long and possible.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Urban roads transitioning to US 40 highway

suffering_zones.label: The Open Farmland
suffering_zones.desc: West of Indianapolis on US 40 and county roads. The city is behind you. Indiana farmland stretches to every horizon. Cornfields as far as you can see. Zero shade. The sun is climbing. The temperature is rising. This is where RAIN begins.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Flat, exposed farmland roads, zero shade

suffering_zones.label: The Halfway Wall
suffering_zones.desc: Roughly halfway. Lunch rest stop. You have ridden 80 miles and you have 80 miles left. The math is simple and unforgiving. The heat is at its peak. The temptation to stop is real. This is the mental crux of RAIN.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Flat farmland, midday heat at peak

suffering_zones.label: The Grind East
suffering_zones.desc: East of Indianapolis. The miles are accumulating. The heat is oppressive. The farmland is endless. You are tired in a way that is different from climbing tired -- you are grinding tired. Every pedal stroke covers the same flat ground, and there are still 40 miles left.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Flat county roads, afternoon heat, potential headwinds

suffering_zones.label: The Final 10
suffering_zones.desc: Approaching Richmond. The finish is close but not yet here. The route enters the outskirts of Richmond. Small-town Indiana welcomes you. 10 miles. 9. 8. Counting down now. The sun is low. You have been riding for 10+ hours.

suffering_zones.label: Earlham College Finish
suffering_zones.desc: The finish at Earlham College in Richmond. Food. A keychain medal. The knowledge that you rode across an entire state in a single day. You started at dawn. It is dusk. Indiana is behind you.

course_description.signature_challenge: The distance itself. 160 miles on flat roads with no descents to rest on, no dramatic terrain to distract you, and Indiana summer heat accumulating over 10-14 hours. The challenge is pure endurance.

guide_variables.race_location: Terre Haute to Richmond, Indiana, USA
guide_variables.race_terrain: Flat paved roads across Indiana farmland
guide_variables.race_weather: Indiana midsummer: 85-95F, high humidity, no shade, thunderstorm risk
race_challenges: 160 miles in a single day on flat roads
Indiana summer heat: 85-95F with high humidity and no shade
No descents to rest on: every mile is pedaled
Point-to-point logistics across the entire state
Mental endurance for 10-14 hours of flat road

non_negotiables.why: 160 miles requires at least two training rides of 100+ miles and regular weekly volume of 150+ miles. You must be comfortable on a bike for 10+ hours. Build your endurance systematically. There are no shortcuts to 160 miles.

10-14 hours in 85-95F Indiana heat with high humidity is a medical risk without proper preparation. Practice riding in heat. Develop a hydration plan that delivers 1-1.5 bottles per hour. Carry electrolyte supplements. Start hydrating days before the ride.

You need to get from Richmond back to Terre Haute (or vice versa). Arrange a personal SAG vehicle, coordinate with riding partners, or pre-book transportation. The ride starts in one city and ends 160 miles away in another. Plan this early.
